question: How often must Critical AI systems undergo adversarial red-team testing?
answer: Critical-risk AI systems shall undergo adversarial red-team testing at least quarterly. [Source: data/documents/current\Synthetic AI Secure Development Lifecycle (AI-SDL) Standard.pdf]

AI Secure Development Lifecycle Standard (AI-SDL) 
Version: 2.0 
Effective Date: January 1, 2026 
Purpose 
This standard establishes minimum security, governance, and control requirements for the 
development, deployment, and monitoring of Generative AI and Agentic AI systems. 
Section 1 – Risk Classification 
AI-SDL-001 
All AI systems shall be classified as Low, Medium, High, or Critical risk prior to production 
deployment. 
AI-SDL-002

question: What guidance currently governs model risk management practices?
answer: The revised model risk management guidance currently governs model risk management practices [Source: data/documents/current\SR2602.pdf]. This guidance was issued on April 9, 2021 [Source: data/documents/current\SR2602.pdf] and updates the model risk management guidance that was in place since the issuance of SR 11-7 [Source: data/documents/current\SR2602.pdf].
contexts:
Act/Anti-Money Laundering Compliance  (issued April 9, 2021). This revised guidance reflects 
supervisory experience and industry feedback accumulated over the past fifteen years, as well as 
significant advancements in modeling practices. 
Based on supervisory experience and industry feedback since the issuance of SR 11-7, the 
agencies have updated the model risk management guidance to clarify model risk management

Corporation (the “agencies”) are issuing this revised model risk management guidance to clarify 
model risk management principles, and to set forth a risk-based approach to model risk 
management, tailored to a banking organization’s model risk profile and the size and complexity 
of its operations. 
This guidance is expected to be most relevant to banking organizations with over $30 
 
1 See 12 CFR Part 4, Subpart F, Appendix A (OCC); 12 CFR Part 262, Appendix A (Board); 12 CFR Part 302,

principles and to emphasize a risk-based approach to model risk management that is tailored to a 
banking organization’s model risk profile and the size and complexity of its operations. The revised 
guidance highlights sound principles for effective model risk management while recognizing that 
model risk management practices appropriately vary among banking organizations based on their 
specific risk profiles and model usage.

question: Should model risk management activities be tailored according to model risk?
answer: Yes, model risk management activities should be tailored according to model risk. A banking organization may tailor portions of its model risk management practices that are relevant to the risks posed by a particular model [Source: data/documents/current\SR2602.pdf]. Not all models present the same level of risk, and practices should be appropriate and effective for managing the specific risks the banking organization faces or is likely to face [Source: data/documents/current\SR2602.pdf].
contexts:
The size and complexity of the banking organization’s operations also may influence the 
range of model risk management. A banking organization with simple operations may not 
conduct model risk management with the same rigor as an organization with relatively more 
complex operations. Furthermore, a banking organization may tailor portions of its model risk 
management practices that are relevant to the risks posed by a particular model and with a level

generally considered to be of greater risk than models that are not used for such purposes.   
Model purpose, together with model exposure, determines model materiality. Assessing 
risk based on model materiality is an important consideration for sound model risk management. 
Banking organizations may deem certain models immaterial based on model exposure and 
purpose. In those cases, model risk management may consist of identifying those models and

risk management practices that are appropriate and effective for managing the specific risks the 
banking organization faces or is likely to face.

management practices. Not all models present the same level of risk, and a similar model at two 
different banking organizations may present different risks. As such, practices that are

question: Which document is the current authoritative model risk management guidance?
answer: The current authoritative model risk management guidance is the Revised Guidance on Model Risk Management (Source: data/documents/current\SR2602.pdf). This revised guidance supersedes and replaces SR letter 11-7, Guidance on Model Risk Management, and SR letter 21-8, Interagency Statement on Model Risk Management for Bank Systems Supporting Bank Secrecy Act/Anti-Money Laundering Compliance (Source: data/documents/current\SR2602.pdf).

letter 11 -7,  Guidance on Model Risk Management  (issued April 4, 2011) and SR letter 21- 8, 
Interagency Statement on Model Risk Management for Bank Systems Supporting Bank Secrecy

question: Does replacing the foundation model require re-validation?
answer: Yes, replacing the foundation model provider requires re-validation prior to production deployment. (Source: data/documents/current\Synthetic AI Secure Development Lifecycle (AI-SDL) Standard.pdf, AI-SDL-006)

When extending a model’s use beyond its original application, sound practice involves 
considering additional analysis of the new usage and its limitations, along with review of 
existing controls to manage the resulting risk.  
V. MODEL VALIDATION AND MONITORING 
Model validation evaluates whether models perform as expected and includes an 
assessment of a model’s reliability and its limitations. The nature and rigor of validation generally

sound practice also involves appropriately documenting, justifying, and evaluating adjustments 
made to customize the model as part of model validation.

question: Should annual red-team testing satisfy current AI-SDL requirements?
answer: No, annual red-team testing would not satisfy current AI-SDL requirements for critical-risk AI systems, as they "shall undergo adversarial red-team testing at least quarterly." [Source: data/documents/current\Synthetic AI Secure Development Lifecycle (AI-SDL) Standard.pdf]
